skin_warnings: B03: composer beat has an empty spark line — SPARK-LINE LAW wants a short serif cue (e.g. "The change,").

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam, in for Bear. This video was made by the skill it explains. The ai-explainer skill — formerly claude-explainer — is the Claude-branded production pipeline for this channel. Every video in the Brutalist meta-series ran through it. So when I was asked to explain it, there was only one honest answer: use it on itself. Let me show you what happened.

props.command: Show me the ai-explainer skill — how does it explain itself?
output: Step 1: Arm the skill-teardown modifier (auto-triggers on any SKILL.md source).
Step 2: Break the skill into anatomy + pipeline + mechanism acts + design tell.
Step 3: Self-demo — this very video is the skill demoing itself.

beats.narration_text: A skill is a folder of instructions Claude reads before it works. The ai-explainer folder is about fourteen kilobytes of SKILL.md. Everything in it: the palette law, the channel table, the ILLUSTRATE law, GATE P, the skill-teardown modifier, the profile modifier — all in one document. Its old name was claude-explainer. It was renamed because vendor names are not allowed as skill identities. The old name still resolves — the art dispatcher has an alias map. The identity changed; the mechanism stayed.

props.title: A skill is a folder.
props.calloutText: Declared inside: channels · palette laws · ILLUSTRATE law · GATE P · modifiers
props.calloutSub: Old name: claude-explainer (renamed: vendor names aren't skill identities)

beats.narration_text: The pipeline has five stages. First: author the beat sheet — every beat's narration in the Teardown register. Second: PEDAGOGY check, the GATE P gate — human signs off before any audio spend. Third: Kokoro audio — each narration beat generates an mp3, its measured duration becomes the clock. Fourth: visuals — Remotion scenes, Manim fragments, code blocks — all conforming to the audio duration. Fifth: compile dot py stitches everything into the final mp4. The key invariant: audio first, always. Never fix timing by hand — regenerate audio, then recompile.

props.title: How the skill builds.

phases.label: PEDAGOGY GATE
phases.desc: GATE P — human signs before spend

props.footerNote: Audio-first: measured durations are the master clock — never hand-time.

beats.narration_text: Let's self-demo. I can't run myself on myself — that gets recursive — so here's the next best thing: watch the skill run on its closest sibling, sketch-explainer. One command. The skill reads sketch-explainer's entire SKILL.md. It authors the beat sheet — eleven beats in the Teardown register. It runs the PEDAGOGY check. Then it queues audio. The ask that triggers all of it: one line at the terminal.

props.segment: Run the skill on its sibling
props.runningText: reading sketch-explainer SKILL.md · authoring beat sheet · PEDAGOGY check…
output: Beat sheet authored: 11 beats · Teardown register · skill-teardown modifier armed.
PEDAGOGY VERDICT: PASS — scored against 6 criteria.
Audio queued: Kokoro am_onyx · no paid spend.

beats.narration_text: And here's the result. A beat_sheet.json. Each beat has three required fields: the beat id, the narration, and the shot definition. The shot definition picks the Remotion scene and its props. The metadata block sets the brand — claude-liam, Kokoro am_onyx, claude palette. That's the machine's output from one command. The human's job starts after this file exists: listen to the audio, judge the narration, watch the frames. The script is the receipt, not the product.

props.title: beat_sheet.json — ai-explainer output

beats.narration_text: The most distinctive law in the skill is the ILLUSTRATE law. The Claude interface appears in exactly five places: cold open, ask micro-beats, verdict page, handoff, and outro. Every other beat has to show something that teaches — a Remotion concept illustration, a Manim fragment, a code block. You're watching it right now: this beat is a mechanism card, not a composer window. The skill's own wording: two consecutive beats on the same visual scheme is the smell, a reel of composer wallpaper is the failure this law exists to prevent.

props.heading: The UI earns its beats.
props.body: Five appearances only: cold open · ask micro-beats · verdict page · handoff · outro. Every other inner beat illustrates its concept — Remotion diagram, Manim fragment, or code block.
props.quote: Two consecutive beats on the same visual scheme is the smell; a reel of composer wallpaper is the failure this law prevents.

beats.narration_text: The skill-teardown modifier is what arms this video. It kicks in automatically whenever the source is a SKILL.md file — you don't invoke it explicitly. What it adds to the body: an anatomy beat, a pipeline beat, two to four mechanism acts, and the design tell. Then the self-demo law: the reel has to use the skill to show the skill. Real output, rebuilt natively in Remotion or Manim. Never a screenshot, never a fabricated demo. The SKILL.md is blunt about it: a faked demo is a DOUBLE-CHECK LAW violation, not a shortcut.

props.heading: The modifier arms automatically.
props.body: Source is a SKILL.md → modifier arms without being named. Body adds: anatomy · pipeline · 2-4 mechanism acts · design tell · self-demo.
props.quote: A faked demo is a DOUBLE-CHECK LAW violation, not a shortcut.

beats.narration_text: The design tell: the fidelity choice. This skill uses the actual Claude app palette — cream page, warm ink, one terracotta accent — not because it's pretty, but because looking like the real product IS the instruction. You're not watching a branded animation; you're watching the tool, in use. The palette doesn't change per episode. The voice does, the register does, the content does. The window stays the same. The SKILL.md calls it a fidelity brand, and the rule is strict: the moment it stops looking like the actual Claude app it loses its reason to exist.

props.heading: Fidelity is the explanation.
props.body: The palette replicates the real Claude app by design. The voice changes. The register changes. The content changes. The window never does.
props.quote: The moment it stops looking like the actual Claude app it loses its reason to exist.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The ai-explainer skill — formerly claude-explainer — is the production pipeline for this channel's Claude-branded content. It runs on three fixed facts: the fidelity palette, the Teardown register, and the ILLUSTRATE law. The skill-teardown modifier arms automatically when the source is a SKILL.md file, adding anatomy, pipeline, mechanism acts, and design tell to the body. The self-demo law requires the reel to actually run the skill — not describe it. This video ran the skill. The palette is fixed. The register is Liam's. The mechanism is in the file.

artifactLines: The ai-explainer skill is the Claude-branded production pipeline: cream page, terracotta spark, Teardown register — applied to any concept or SKILL.md.
The skill-teardown modifier arms automatically on any SKILL.md source — anatomy + pipeline + mechanism acts + design tell + self-demo where feasible.
ILLUSTRATE LAW governs the middle: the UI earns exactly five appearances; every other beat illustrates its concept natively.
Fidelity is the explanation — the palette replicates the real Claude app by design; the moment it drifts it loses its reason to exist.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here's the prompt — read it with me. 'Use the ai-explainer skill on a SKILL.md you've written or found — pick one mechanism that surprises you and build a teardown episode around it. Start with: what does this skill ACTUALLY do that its description doesn't say?' That last clause is the Teardown law at work: the description is the README; the mechanism is what you find when you actually read the file. Read it all the way through, find the line that doesn't belong in a summary — that's your episode. Run this in Claude Code, point it at a real SKILL.md. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Use the ai-explainer skill on a SKILL.md you've written or found — pick one mechanism that surprises you and build a teardown episode around it. Start with: what does this skill ACTUALLY do that its description doesn't say?

beats.narration_text: Claude, Self-Taught. Liam, in for Bear.
